title: How Big Is the Universe? – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: The UNIVERSE is everything we can see and know about in space. It includes all the STARS, PLANETS, MOONS, and even Earth where we live. The UNIVERSE is so incredibly BIG that it is hard to imagine how large it really is. Scientists use special TELESCOPES to look out into space and learn more about the UNIVERSE. When you look up at the night sky, you are seeing just a tiny, tiny piece of the whole UNIVERSE.

questions.question: What do scientists use to look out into space and learn about the universe?
Name some things the text tells us are part of the universe.
Imagine you are lying on your back looking up at the night sky. What do you see twinkling above you?
When you look at the night sky, what tiny piece are you seeing?
questions.answer: A TINY PIECE OF THE WHOLE UNIVERSE

hunks.text: Our SOLAR SYSTEM is one small part of the UNIVERSE. The SUN sits in the middle of our SOLAR SYSTEM, and PLANETS like Earth, Jupiter, and Mars orbit around it. Our SOLAR SYSTEM is like a family of objects all moving together in space. Even though our SOLAR SYSTEM seems really big to us, it is just one tiny neighborhood in the enormous UNIVERSE. If you imagine the UNIVERSE as a huge city, our SOLAR SYSTEM would be like just one house on one street.

questions.question: What sits in the middle of our solar system?
What planets does the text tell us orbit around the Sun?
What do we call the path that planets take around the Sun?
Picture our solar system as a family of objects all moving together. What do you imagine them doing?
questions.answer: MOVING / SPINNING / ORBITING / TRAVELING THROUGH SPACE

hunks.text: Beyond our SOLAR SYSTEM, there are billions and billions of STARS scattered throughout space. Each STAR might have its own planets and moons around it, just like our SUN does. Scientists call groups of STARS that stick together GALAXIES. A GALAXY is like a giant city of STARS all spinning together in space. Our GALAXY is called the MILKY WAY, and it contains over 100 BILLION STARS—more STARS than there are grains of sand on all the beaches on Earth!

questions.question: What do scientists call groups of stars that stick together?
What is the name of our galaxy?
How many stars does the Milky Way contain?
How do you feel when you think about 100 billion stars in just one galaxy?

hunks.text: Just like our GALAXY has billions of STARS, scientists have discovered that there are billions of GALAXIES in the UNIVERSE. Each GALAXY is separated from the next one by huge empty spaces called SPACE. When scientists look through powerful TELESCOPES, they see GALAXIES that are so far away that the light from their STARS takes millions of years to reach us. This means we are actually seeing light from STARS that burned out a very long time ago. The UNIVERSE keeps stretching farther and farther, and we have not found the end of it yet.

questions.question: What separates one galaxy from the next galaxy?
What are two things the text tells us about light from distant stars?
questions.answer: IT TAKES MILLIONS OF YEARS TO REACH US / WE SEE LIGHT FROM STARS THAT BURNED OUT A LONG TIME AGO

questions.question: What is another name for how long it takes light to travel from a distant star to Earth?
Imagine looking through a telescope and watching light travel across billions of miles of space. What does that journey feel like?

hunks.text: To understand how big the UNIVERSE really is, scientists compare it to things we know. If Earth were the size of a grain of sand, our whole SOLAR SYSTEM would fit inside a building. Our GALAXY would stretch across an entire city. And the whole UNIVERSE—with all its billions of GALAXIES—would be bigger than anything we could ever build or imagine. The UNIVERSE is so BIG that even with the fastest SPACESHIP we have today, it would take longer than the age of the UNIVERSE itself to travel from one side to the other!

questions.question: If Earth were the size of a grain of sand, how big would our whole solar system be?
questions.answer: IT WOULD FIT INSIDE A BUILDING

questions.question: What are two size comparisons the text makes to help us understand the universe?
questions.answer: EARTH AS A GRAIN OF SAND / SOLAR SYSTEM INSIDE A BUILDING / GALAXY ACROSS A CITY / UNIVERSE BIGGER THAN ANYTHING WE COULD BUILD

questions.question: Close your eyes and imagine how long it would take our fastest spaceship to travel across the entire universe. What does that endless journey feel like in your mind?
Could our fastest spaceship today travel all the way across the universe?
questions.answer: NO / IT WOULD TAKE LONGER THAN THE AGE OF THE UNIVERSE ITSELF

hunks.text: Scientists discovered something amazing about the UNIVERSE: it is still GROWING! Long ago, the entire UNIVERSE started very, very small in something called the BIG BANG. Since then, it has been getting bigger and bigger, spreading out in all directions. The UNIVERSE is still EXPANDING today, which means new SPACE is being created all the time. Distant GALAXIES are moving away from us faster and faster. Even though we cannot see it happening, we live in a UNIVERSE that is constantly changing and getting larger.

questions.question: What is the name for the event when the entire universe started very small?
Is the universe still growing today?
What does the text tell us is happening to distant galaxies?
questions.answer: THEY ARE MOVING AWAY FROM US / THEY ARE MOVING FASTER AND FASTER

questions.question: What does it make you think about when you learn that the whole universe is still growing and changing?

hunks.text: Even though the UNIVERSE is impossibly BIG, humans are curious explorers who want to understand it. We send ROBOTS and SATELLITES into space to learn more about distant PLANETS and STARS. ASTRONOMERS—scientists who study space—use TELESCOPES to see farther and deeper into the UNIVERSE than ever before. Every year, scientists discover new GALAXIES and learn new facts about how the UNIVERSE works. Our curiosity about the UNIVERSE teaches us that even the smallest person on Earth can ask big questions and help solve big mysteries.

questions.question: What do we call scientists who study space?
Name two things the text says humans send into space to learn more.
What is a tool that astronomers use to see distant stars and galaxies?
Imagine you are an astronomer looking through a powerful telescope at a distant galaxy for the first time. What new things do you discover?
questions.answer: NEW STARS / NEW PLANETS / NEW GALAXIES / BEAUTIFUL LIGHTS / MYSTERIES

hunks.text: Learning about the BIG UNIVERSE helps us feel both very small and very special at the same time. We are tiny specks living on one small PLANET in a vast ocean of SPACE, yet we are smart enough to understand amazing things about it! The UNIVERSE is so BIG and so old that scientists are still discovering new STARS, PLANETS, and GALAXIES. Even though the UNIVERSE might seem too BIG to understand, every person who looks up at the night sky and wonders is part of the grand adventure of exploring it. Your questions about the UNIVERSE matter, and they help scientists learn more about our amazing cosmic home.

questions.question: What two opposite feelings does learning about the big universe give us?
What are things scientists are still discovering in the universe?
questions.answer: NEW STARS / NEW PLANETS / NEW GALAXIES

questions.question: If you could ask the universe one big question, what would you want to know?
Picture yourself on a dark night looking up at all the stars. How does it feel to know you are part of something so enormous and beautiful?

citations: NASA's Space Place: 'How Big Is the Universe?' (spaceplace.nasa.gov)
National Geographic Kids: 'Universe Facts for Kids' (kids.nationalgeographic.com)
Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum: 'Understanding the Universe' (airandspace.si.edu)
